file_name: Minarno et al. - 2024 - Classification of skin cancer images using convolutional neural network with ResNet50 pre-trained mo.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a pre-trained ResNet50 architecture and transfer learning to classify seven classes of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved an overall accuracy of 87.42%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using a pre-trained ResNet50 model on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (Materials and Methods, Results and Discussion), reports new quantitative results (87.42% accuracy), and compares its performance against previous studies, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Classification of Skin Cancer Images Using Convolutional Neural Network with ResNet50 Pre-trained Model
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of seven distinct skin cancer lesion classes using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a pre-trained ResNet50 architecture. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains over 10,000 dermoscopic images. To mitigate class imbalance, the researchers employed data augmentation techniques such as rotation, flipping, and zooming during the training process. The model was optimized using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 and trained for 100 epochs. The proposed ResNet50 model achieved an overall accuracy of 87.42%, outperforming previous MobileNet-based studies on the same dataset. Additionally, the model demonstrated high reliability with Top2 and Top3 accuracy rates of 95.52% and 97.86%, respectively, and perfect precision, recall, and F1-scores for Melanoma classification.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin cancer lesion classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Vascular Skin Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on classifying dermoscopic images into one of seven discrete diagnostic categories using a CNN with ResNet50. The task is multi-class classification, evaluated using categorical accuracy, Top2/Top3 accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score per class.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images obtained from the Kaggle platform, containing high-resolution images of skin lesions across seven distinct categories.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet50 model with data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming) to mitigate class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for model validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation and performance assessment (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).
use.other: The model was pre-trained on ImageNet before being fine-tuned on this dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and compared qualitatively to previous studies.

HAM10000.notes: The ResNet50 model outperformed the MobileNet baseline in categorical accuracy (87.42% vs 83.15%), Top2 accuracy (95.52% vs 91.36%), and Top3 accuracy (97.86% vs 95.86%). The model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-score for Melanoma (Mel). Challenges were noted for Dermatofibroma (DF) and Melanocytic Nevi (NV) with lower recall scores. Misclassifications were observed between Benign Keratosis (BKL) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset, then fine-tuned on HAM10000 dataset using transfer learning.

architecture_details.key_features: Innovative use of residual connections (skip connections) to overcome vanishing gradient issues and learn shallow and deep features.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from 600x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels to match ResNet50 input requirements
Data splitting into training (9,077 images) and validation (938 images) sets
Data augmentation using ImageDataGenerator in Keras, including rotation (height_shift_range: 0.1), zooming (zoom_range: 0.1), horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping
conclusions: The proposed ResNet50 pre-trained model achieved an overall categorical accuracy of 87.42% in classifying seven skin cancer lesion classes using the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming the previous MobileNet baseline accuracy of 83.15%.
The model demonstrated high reliability with Top2 and Top3 accuracy rates of 95.52% and 97.86%, respectively.
The model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-score (1.00) for the Melanoma (MEL) class, indicating its strong capability in identifying this life-threatening lesion.
The model exhibited lower recall for Dermatofibroma (DF) and Melanocytic Nevi (NV) classes, highlighting specific limitations in distinguishing these classes.
Misclassifications were observed between Benign Keratosis (BKL) and Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), likely due to overlapping visual features.
Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, zooming) were effective in mitigating class imbalance and improving the model's generalizability.

The study suggests that future improvements could involve targeted data augmentation, dataset rebalancing, advanced loss functions (e.g., focal loss), or ensemble learning approaches to address classification challenges for specific classes.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance and demographic biases (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse skin types.

The model achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-score (1.00) for Melanoma, which may indicate data leakage or overfitting, as perfect scores on a public dataset are unusually high and often suggest that validation/test samples were inadvertently included in training or that the split was not strictly independent.

The paper compares the proposed ResNet50 model only against a previous MobileNet study (Chaturvedi et al. [24]) rather than establishing a robust baseline within the same experimental setup, making the performance comparison less rigorous.

There is a discrepancy in the reported training epochs: the abstract and methods section mention 100 epochs, while the results section text mentions 50 epochs, creating ambiguity about the final training duration.

The dataset split resulted in a very small validation set for certain classes (e.g., only 11 samples for Dermatofibroma and 13 for Vascular Lesions in the validation set), which may lead to unreliable performance metrics for these minority classes.
The study does not report any external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about the model's clinical applicability and robustness across different imaging devices and populations.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the improvement over the baseline MobileNet model.

The model exhibited low recall for Dermatofibroma (0.60) and Melanocytic Nevi (0.533), indicating a high rate of false negatives for these classes, which is a critical limitation for diagnostic reliability.
